_meta.title: PediaDose — Condition-to-Drug Mapping
sources: KKH Women's & Children's Hospital, Department of Emergency Medicine, Clinical Guidelines, January 2026 Edition (revised 4 Mar 2026)
NHG Pharmacy Paediatric Dose Calculator, Reviewed June 2024 (Version 2024.07.26)
_meta.warning: For clinical decision support only. All dose values must be verified by a qualified clinician against current formulary guidelines before use with real patients. Not for use in neonates (under 1 month old) unless neonatal dose is explicitly stated.

conditions.name: Asthma
conditions.notes: Assess severity before prescribing. Children with severe asthma attacks may not appear distressed — do not under-treat based on appearance alone.

severityTiers.label: Mild
severityTiers.description: Breathless walking, talks in sentences, no accessory muscle use, HR <100, SpO2 >95%

drugs.clinicalNote: Use spacer with face mask for children under 5 years. Spacer without face mask for children 5 years and older.

severityTiers.label: Moderate to severe
severityTiers.description: Breathless talking/at rest, speaks in phrases, moderate-severe retractions, HR 100–200, SpO2 91–95% (moderate) or <91% (severe)

drugs.dose_instruction: MDI Salbutamol: <10 kg = 5 puffs per cycle; >10 kg = 10 puffs per cycle
drugs.frequency: Up to 2–3 cycles, review after each
drugs.clinicalNote: Give back-to-back cycles if severity is moderate or above.

drugs.dose_instruction: MDI Ipratropium: <10 kg = 2 puffs per cycle; >10 kg = 4 puffs per cycle
drugs.clinicalNote: Always combine with Salbutamol for moderate/severe. Do not give alone.
Start as early as possible — takes at least 4 hours to act. Indicated for: exacerbation >48h, moderate-severe, past history of severe episode.

severityTiers.label: Severe (SpO2 <90%) — nebulised route
severityTiers.description: SpO2 <90%, use nebulised route. Breathless at rest, infant stops feeding, severe retractions, audible wheeze, HR >200.

drugs.dose_instruction: Nebulised Salbutamol 0.5% undiluted continuous: <10 kg = 0.5 mL; >10 kg = 1 mL
drugs.frequency: Continuous with at least 6 L/min O2
drugs.clinicalNote: Use only when SpO2 <90%. Requires supplemental oxygen at ≥6 L/min.

drugs.dose_instruction: Nebulised Ipratropium 0.025%: <10 kg = 0.5 mL; >10 kg = 1 mL

drugs.clinicalNote: Start as early as possible.

conditions.name: Croup
conditions.notes: Severity assessed using Westley Croup Score. Score ≤2 = mild; 3–7 = moderate; >7 = severe.

severityTiers.label: Mild (Westley score ≤2)
severityTiers.description: Non-toxic, well hydrated, no stridor at rest

drugs.frequency: Stat dose. If croup score ≥2 at 2h review, give additional 0.45 mg/kg.
drugs.clinicalNote: Review at 2 hours. Discharge if croup score <2 after review. Max 3 doses, 12h apart.

severityTiers.label: Moderate (Westley score 3–7)
severityTiers.description: Stridor at rest, mild to moderate agitation

drugs.clinicalNote: Add nebulised adrenaline if score 4 or above. Review hourly. Max 3 doses, 12h apart.

drugs.dose_instruction: Nebulised Adrenaline (1:1000) 3 mL + 1 mL normal saline
drugs.fixed_dose_rule: 3 mL adrenaline (1:1000) + 1 mL normal saline (fixed dose)
drugs.frequency: PRN, may repeat. Monitor for rebound.
drugs.clinicalNote: Add for score ≥4. Observe for minimum 3–4 hours post-nebulisation before discharge.

severityTiers.label: Severe (Westley score >7)
severityTiers.description: Severe stridor at rest, severe respiratory distress, SpO2 <95%

drugs.clinicalNote: ADMIT to CICU. Consider intubation in CE if altered sensorium, persistent cyanosis, or inability to maintain saturations. Max 3 doses, 12h apart.

drugs.dose_instruction: Nebulised Adrenaline (1:1000) 3 mL + 1 mL normal saline
drugs.fixed_dose_rule: 3 mL adrenaline (1:1000) + 1 mL normal saline (fixed dose)

conditions.name: Febrile seizure
conditions.notes: Distinguish simple (age 6m–6yr, <15 min, generalised, single episode in 24h) from complex (>15 min, focal, or recurrent within 24h). If seizure is ongoing, refer to Status Epilepticus pathway.

severityTiers.label: Post-ictal (seizure stopped)
severityTiers.description: Child has stopped seizing. Provide antipyretics for comfort. Monitor for recurrence.

drugs.clinicalNote: Antipyretics will not prevent recurrence of febrile seizure but improve comfort.
Alternate with paracetamol if needed. Suitable for children ≥6 months, >5 kg.

severityTiers.label: Active / recurrent seizure
severityTiers.description: Child is actively seizing or seizure recurs. Treat to abort seizure.

drugs.frequency: Once IV; may repeat once if no response after 5 min
drugs.clinicalNote: Preferred if IV access available. Give slowly.

drugs.dose_instruction: 0.4 mg/kg PR: use 0.5 vial (2.5 mg) if weight <10 kg; 1 vial (5 mg) if >10 kg
drugs.frequency: Once; may repeat once after 5 min if no IV access
drugs.clinicalNote: Use if no IV access. Do not delay treatment waiting for IV access.

conditions.name: Status epilepticus
conditions.notes: Follow clinical pathway (Fig 1.1). Check glucose — if <3 mmol/L give 5 mL/kg IV dextrose 10%.

severityTiers.label: First-line (0–5 min)
severityTiers.description: Seizure ongoing. Establish airway. Check glucose.

drugs.frequency: Once IV. Repeat once after 5 min if seizure persists.
drugs.clinicalNote: Preferred if IV access available.

drugs.frequency: Alternative to lorazepam if lorazepam unavailable

drugs.clinicalNote: Gain IV access while administering.

severityTiers.label: Second-line (5–20 min, seizure persists)
severityTiers.description: Seizure continues after first-line benzodiazepine.

drugs.clinicalNote: Preferred second-line agent.

drugs.frequency: Once, over 20–30 min. Max infusion rate 1 mg/kg/min. Consider 5–10 mg/kg if already on phenobarbitone.
drugs.clinicalNote: Dilute with normal saline. Monitor BP.

drugs.frequency: Once, over 20 min. Max infusion rate 1 mg/kg/min.
drugs.clinicalNote: CAUTION: Do not use if cardiac risk factors or known SCN1A/SCN5A mutations. Monitor ECG and BP. Do not give if patient already on phenytoin maintenance.

conditions.name: Urinary tract infection
conditions.source: KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026, p.110–117; NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024
conditions.notes: Febrile UTI: treat empirically for 10–14 days. Afebrile UTI: treat 5–7 days. Not for use in children <3 months — admit and discuss with senior.

severityTiers.label: Febrile UTI (age ≥3 months, uncomplicated, outpatient)
severityTiers.description: Child ≥3 months, febrile, no sepsis, tolerating oral feeds, no renal tract abnormality

drugs.clinicalNote: First-line for febrile UTI at KKH CE. Follow up urine C&S. Return if fever persists >48h on antibiotics.
Alternative to cephalexin. Dose 80–90 mg/kg/day based on amoxicillin component. KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026.

severityTiers.label: Afebrile UTI (lower tract, outpatient)
severityTiers.description: No fever, lower tract symptoms only (dysuria, frequency)

drugs.clinicalNote: Per KKH CE guidelines. No prophylaxis needed unless underlying renal abnormality.

conditions.name: Anaphylaxis
conditions.notes: IM adrenaline is FIRST-LINE. Do not delay for any reason. Adjuvant treatments (antihistamine, salbutamol, steroid) must NOT delay adrenaline.

severityTiers.label: Acute anaphylaxis — all cases
severityTiers.description: Acute onset with skin/mucosal involvement AND respiratory compromise OR hypotension OR severe GI symptoms. Or acute hypotension/bronchospasm/laryngeal involvement after known allergen exposure.

drugs.frequency: Stat IM. May repeat every 15 min if required.
drugs.clinicalNote: FIRST-LINE. Must be given immediately. Do not delay for IV access or other medications. EpiPen weight banding: 7.5–25 kg → EpiPen Jr 0.15 mg IM; >25 kg → EpiPen 0.3 mg IM. Do NOT give IV.
Adjuvant — only after adrenaline. For bronchospasm component.
2nd-generation non-sedating antihistamine. Give only after patient stabilised with IM adrenaline. Do NOT use sedating 1st-generation antihistamines (e.g. diphenhydramine) routinely.

conditions.name: Pneumonia (community-acquired)
conditions.source: KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026, p.178–184; NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024
conditions.notes: CXR findings and age guide antibiotic choice. Viral pneumonia (common in <3 years with normal WCC) may not require antibiotics. Severe pneumonia requires admission — outpatient management not appropriate.

severityTiers.label: Age 3 months–5 years, CXR consolidation and/or raised WCC
severityTiers.description: Consolidation on CXR, fever, tachypnoea. Non-toxic, suitable for outpatient.

drugs.clinicalNote: High-dose amoxicillin is first-line for typical CAP in this age group. KKH CE 2023.
Alternative to amoxicillin — use if no response at 48h or suspected beta-lactamase-producing organism.

severityTiers.label: Age 3 months–5 years, interstitial changes on CXR, normal/low WCC
severityTiers.description: Likely viral. Antibiotics may not be required. Discuss with senior if concerns.

drugs.frequency: TDS for 5 days (if prescribing — discuss with senior)
drugs.clinicalNote: Most viral LRTIs with peribronchial cuffing do not require antibiotics. Limit to 5 days only if physician concern. No antibiotics if no radiologic lobar consolidation and no significant co-morbidities.

severityTiers.label: Age 5–15 years, interstitial / atypical pattern (suspect Mycoplasma)
severityTiers.description: Non-toxic, CXR interstitial changes, normal/low WCC — likely Mycoplasma in this age group.

drugs.clinicalNote: Macrolide for suspected Mycoplasma, H. influenzae, M. Catarrhalis. Do not combine dual amoxicillin + clarithromycin.

severityTiers.label: Age 5–15 years, consolidation on CXR and/or raised WCC
severityTiers.description: Typical bacterial pneumonia pattern.

drugs.clinicalNote: If no response at 48h, escalate to Augmentin or Clarithromycin (discuss with senior).
Alternative to amoxicillin — use if no response at 48h or suspected beta-lactamase-producing organism.

conditions.name: Skin and soft tissue infection
conditions.source: KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026, p.346; NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024
conditions.notes: Distinguish between impetigo/ecthyma (superficial) and cellulitis/erysipelas (deeper). Toxic or orbital cellulitis requires admission.

severityTiers.label: Impetigo / ecthyma (superficial)
severityTiers.description: Honey-crusted lesions, no systemic features

drugs.clinicalNote: KKH CE 2023: Cephalexin 50 mg/kg/day TDS for 1 week. Lexicomp: 25–50 mg/kg/day q6–8h.

severityTiers.label: Cellulitis / erysipelas (non-toxic, non-orbital)
severityTiers.description: Spreading skin erythema, warmth, non-toxic, manageable as outpatient

drugs.clinicalNote: KKH CE guideline first choice for skin infections. For ≤20 kg: 25–50 mg/kg/day div 6h.
Alternative to cloxacillin. KKH CE 2023: 50 mg/kg/day q8h for 1 week.
For animal/human bite wounds or mixed polymicrobial infection. Not first-line for simple cellulitis per KKH CE — use cloxacillin or cephalexin instead.

conditions.name: Tonsillitis / pharyngitis
conditions.source: KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026, p.346; NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024
conditions.notes: Treat only if Group A Streptococcus (GAS) suspected or confirmed. Most viral pharyngitis does not require antibiotics.

severityTiers.label: Group A Strep pharyngitis / tonsillitis
severityTiers.description: Fever, exudative tonsils, anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, no cough (Centor criteria). Rapid Strep test or clinical diagnosis.

drugs.clinicalNote: KKH CE / Lexicomp: 50 mg/kg/day q12h for 10 days. Max 1000 mg/day.
Alternative to amoxicillin. KKH CE: penicillin V 50 mg/kg/day q6–8h for 10 days.
For penicillin-allergic patients. Lexicomp: 7.5 mg/kg/dose q12h for 10 days; max 250 mg/dose.

conditions.name: Otitis media
conditions.source: KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026; NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024 — Indication Specific Antibiotics sheet
conditions.notes: High-dose amoxicillin recommended for AOM. Consider watchful waiting in children ≥2 years with mild symptoms and no severe otalgia.

severityTiers.label: Acute otitis media (confirmed or suspected)
severityTiers.description: Fever, ear pain, bulging tympanic membrane. <2 years: treat 10 days. ≥2 years: treat 7 days or watchful waiting if mild.

drugs.frequency: BD — 10 days if <2 years; 7 days if ≥2 years
drugs.clinicalNote: High-dose amoxicillin is preferred first-line. KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026.

drugs.frequency: BD — 10 days if <2 years; 7 days if ≥2 years
drugs.clinicalNote: Alternative to amoxicillin. Use if no response or recurrent AOM. Dose based on amoxicillin component.

severityTiers.label: Perforated acute otitis media
severityTiers.description: Ear discharge through perforated tympanic membrane

drugs.clinicalNote: Oral antibiotics plus topical ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops TDS for 5 days. KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026.
Alternative to amoxicillin. Also add ciprofloxacin 0.3% ear drops TDS 5 days. Dose based on amoxicillin component.

conditions.name: Bronchiolitis
conditions.notes: Bronchiolitis is primarily supportive. Bronchodilators are NOT routine. A trial may be considered in moderate/severe disease with significant personal or family history of atopy — document response clearly.

severityTiers.label: Supportive management (all cases)
severityTiers.description: Clinical diagnosis. Usually in infants <2 years. Supportive care is mainstay.

drugs.dose_instruction: Iliadin nose drops: 0.01% for <1 year old; 0.025% for 1–2 years old
drugs.clinicalNote: Nasal saline/decongestant for symptom relief. Minimal handling to avoid agitation.

severityTiers.label: Optional bronchodilator trial (moderate/severe with atopy history)
severityTiers.description: Only if significant personal or family history of atopy AND moderate or severe disease. Must document response.

drugs.dose_instruction: MDI Salbutamol 0.3 puffs/kg (max 8 puffs) — trial only
drugs.frequency: Single trial. Discontinue if no clear response.
drugs.clinicalNote: IMPORTANT: Bronchodilator is NOT routine for bronchiolitis. Trial only in selected cases. Reassess after each dose and document response clearly. Do not continue if no benefit.

conditions.name: Gastroenteritis
conditions.notes: Primarily supportive — oral rehydration therapy (ORS) is first-line. Antibiotics generally NOT recommended (mostly viral). Ondansetron single dose may reduce vomiting and lower IV rehydration rates.

severityTiers.label: Anti-emetic for vomiting (single dose only)
severityTiers.description: Child vomiting, unable to tolerate ORS. Single dose ondansetron in ED.

drugs.route: Oral (syrup 4 mg/5 mL) or IV if unable to tolerate oral
drugs.clinicalNote: SINGLE DOSE ONLY — do not prescribe as discharge medication. Multiple doses risk QT prolongation. If unable to tolerate oral, may give IV.

severityTiers.label: Fever and discomfort management
severityTiers.description: Symptomatic treatment for fever accompanying gastroenteritis

conditions.name: Fever / pain (general)
conditions.source: KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026, p.9–11; NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024
conditions.notes: Fever defined as ≥38.0°C. Antipyretics treat discomfort — they do not reduce risk of febrile seizure recurrence.

severityTiers.label: Mild to moderate fever / pain
severityTiers.description: Pain score 4–5 or fever ≥38°C, child ≥1 year

drugs.frequency: Q4–6H PRN. Max 4 doses in 24 hours.
drugs.clinicalNote: First-line for fever and mild-moderate pain. KKH CE: nurse-initiated at pain score ≥4.
Suitable for ≥6 months, >5 kg. Avoid in dengue fever, renal impairment, active GI bleeding.

conditions.name: Rhinosinusitis
conditions.source: NHG Pharmacy Calculator Jul 2024 — Indication Specific Antibiotics sheet; KKH CE Guidelines Jan 2026
conditions.notes: Persistent/severe fever and prolonged purulent nasal discharge suggest need for antibiotics. Monitor for complications (orbital cellulitis, meningitis).

severityTiers.label: Standard dose (age ≥2 years)
severityTiers.description: Non-toxic, acute sinusitis

drugs.clinicalNote: Standard-dose regimen. Lexicomp: 45 mg/kg/day q12h.

severityTiers.label: High dose (age ≥2 years, severe or failed standard)
severityTiers.description: Severe symptoms or failed standard-dose treatment

drugs.clinicalNote: High-dose regimen: 80–90 mg/kg/day q12h. Max 2000 mg/dose.
Alternative high-dose option. Dose by amoxicillin component.

conditions.name: Dengue fever
conditions.notes: Dengue is managed supportively. No specific antiviral. AVOID NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, aspirin) — risk of bleeding. Paracetamol only for fever control.

severityTiers.label: Supportive management
severityTiers.description: Fever, myalgia, rash, thrombocytopaenia. Maintain hydration. Monitor FBC, haematocrit.

drugs.clinicalNote: PARACETAMOL ONLY — do not use NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, aspirin, mefenamic acid) in dengue due to risk of bleeding.

conditions.name: Hand, foot and mouth disease
conditions.notes: HFMD is self-limiting. No specific antiviral. Supportive care only. Educate parents on self-limiting nature. Refer immediately if encephalitis features (reduced consciousness, myoclonus, ataxia, seizure, cardiorespiratory instability).

severityTiers.label: Supportive management (uncomplicated)
severityTiers.description: Oral ulcers, vesicular rash on palms/soles, fever. Well child.

drugs.frequency: Q4–6H PRN for fever/pain from ulcers
drugs.clinicalNote: Symptom relief only. No antibiotics required. Advise adequate hydration.
For children ≥6 months. May help with pain from oral ulcers.

conditions.name: Preseptal cellulitis
conditions.notes: Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis is anterior to the orbital septum. Orbital cellulitis is posterior — requires admission and IV antibiotics. Distinguish by proptosis, restricted eye movement, pain on eye movement, or vision changes.

severityTiers.label: Preseptal cellulitis (non-toxic, outpatient)
severityTiers.description: Eyelid swelling and erythema, no proptosis, no restricted eye movement, no fever requiring admission

drugs.clinicalNote: KKH CE first-line for preseptal cellulitis. Review in 24–48h. If worsening or no response, admit for IV antibiotics. Dose based on amoxicillin component.
